## ModalVR Studio

# The Role of Storytelling in VR: How Narrative Shapes Immersive Experiences

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3csvg%20xmlns=%27http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%27%20version=%271.1%27%20width=%27100%27%20height=%27100%27/%3e)
![Kieran Walton](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAAAAAP///yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7 "Kieran Walton")
![Kieran Walton](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Flh3.googleusercontent.com%2Fa%2FAEdFTp41Mhclv6IPZcEp-IRXpei2u1bkZbIg4zmsiWUyiw%3Ds96-c&w=480&q=90 "Kieran Walton")

## The Power of Storytelling in Virtual Reality

In recent years, virtual reality (VR) has emerged as a revolutionary medium, transforming how we interact with digital content. At the heart of this transformation lies the powerful role of storytelling. As VR technology advances, narratives become essential in crafting immersive experiences that captivate and engage users on a profound level.

Storytelling in VR is not just about entertainment; it's about creating a connection between the user and the virtual world. By integrating narrative elements, VR experiences can evoke emotions, build empathy, and offer a sense of presence that traditional media struggles to achieve.

![virtual reality storytelling](https://media.gettyimages.com/id/2189683368/photo/collage-of-thoughts-hand-holding-smartphone-with-halftone-speech-bubbles-digital-arts.jpg?b=1&s=2048x2048&w=0&k=20&c=9V2AOn_004J6z4D8eLXRaza8HU1GgnnqlDLolB5Kg2Y=)

### Enhancing Immersion Through Narrative

One of the primary benefits of storytelling in VR is its ability to enhance immersion. A well-crafted narrative provides context and meaning to the virtual environment, allowing users to feel like active participants rather than passive observers. This immersion is achieved by combining visual, auditory, and interactive elements with a compelling storyline.

For instance, a VR experience set in a post-apocalyptic world becomes more engaging when users follow a storyline that leads them through challenges and discoveries. The narrative structure guides users, making their interactions purposeful and meaningful.

### Creating Emotional Engagement

Emotional engagement is another critical aspect of storytelling in VR. By leveraging narrative techniques such as character development and plot twists, creators can elicit strong emotional responses from users. These emotions can range from excitement and joy to fear and sorrow, depending on the story's direction.

![emotional engagement](https://media.gettyimages.com/id/2151354932/photo/bride-and-groom-with-engagement-ring-close-up.jpg?b=1&s=2048x2048&w=0&k=20&c=RBPhjkYWQN72ihX3rwQOP-YFG7A5QjAPFpwXw7gAkdg=)

Emotions play a significant role in how users remember and relate to their VR experiences. A powerful narrative can leave a lasting impact, making the experience more memorable and meaningful.

### The Role of Interactivity

Interactivity is a fundamental component of VR storytelling. Unlike traditional media, VR allows users to influence the outcome of the story through their actions and decisions. This interactivity fosters a sense of agency, making users feel like they are co-creators of the narrative.

### Challenges and Opportunities

Despite its potential, integrating storytelling into VR presents several challenges. Creating a seamless blend of narrative and interactivity requires careful planning and design. Developers must balance the need for user agency with the constraints of the storyline to avoid breaking immersion.

![vr development challenges](https://media.gettyimages.com/id/2174735603/photo/businessman-exploring-virtual-reality-with-vr-headset-in-modern-office-setting-while.jpg?b=1&s=2048x2048&w=0&k=20&c=4t72MOhjeEqaq0dLtvo80pB4iQQ7tPguPIoy07dgOQA=)

However, these challenges also present opportunities for innovation. As technology progresses, new tools and techniques are emerging to help creators craft more dynamic and immersive narratives. The future of VR storytelling holds immense potential for those willing to explore its possibilities.

### The Future of Storytelling in VR

As VR continues to evolve, storytelling will play an increasingly vital role in shaping immersive experiences. By harnessing the power of narrative, creators can push the boundaries of what is possible in virtual environments, offering users unparalleled opportunities for exploration and emotional connection.

Ultimately, the integration of storytelling into VR represents a new frontier in digital content creation. As we continue to explore this exciting medium, the possibilities for crafting transformative experiences are limited only by our imagination.

What role does storytelling play in enhancing VR experiences?

# What role does storytelling play in enhancing VR experiences?

Storytelling in VR enhances experiences by providing structure, emotional engagement, and guidance. Unlike traditional media, VR places users inside a 3D environment, which can overwhelm them without a clear narrative framework. A well-crafted story directs attention, establishes context, and helps users understand their role in the virtual world. For example, a horror VR game might use environmental cues—like a flickering light leading players down a hallway—to advance the plot while maintaining immersion. Without such guidance, users might wander aimlessly, missing key interactions or becoming disoriented. Storytelling also creates stakes, motivating users to explore and engage with the environment to uncover plot points or resolve conflicts.

From a technical perspective, integrating storytelling requires careful spatial and temporal design. Developers can use triggers tied to user movement or object interactions to advance the narrative. For instance, entering a specific room might activate a voiceover explaining backstory, or picking up an item could unlock a flashback scene. Tools like Unity’s Timeline or Unreal Engine’s Sequencer allow developers to choreograph these events without disrupting interactivity. Additionally, dynamic storytelling—where user choices alter the narrative—can be implemented through branching dialogue systems or event flags. However, this demands robust state management to track variables like inventory items or completed objectives. Balancing scripted moments with user agency is critical; overly rigid narratives feel restrictive, while too much freedom risks diluting the story’s impact.

Practical applications show how storytelling elevates VR beyond pure simulation. Training simulations, for example, use narrative scenarios to teach decision-making under pressure. A medical VR app might place users in an emergency room where they must diagnose a patient while a timer counts down, with the story emphasizing consequences of mistakes. In educational VR, historical reenactments let users witness events firsthand, such as participating in a civil rights march, with the narrative providing context for the era’s social dynamics. Even non-linear experiences benefit from storytelling: exploration-based apps like *Moss* use environmental storytelling—ruins, journals, or symbolic imagery—to build lore without explicit exposition. For developers, prioritizing clear narrative goals during level design and scripting ensures that storytelling complements—rather than competes with—the technical possibilities of VR.

# Designing Emotion through Animation in Virtual Reality - Creating Immersive Experiences

Explore techniques for infusing emotion into animation within virtual reality, creating deeply immersive experiences that resonate with users on a personal level.

![Designing Emotion through Animation in Virtual Reality - Creating Immersive Experiences](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fmoldstud.com%2Fuploads%2Fimages%2Fdesigning-emotion-through-animation-in-virtual-reality-creating-immers.webp%3Fw%3D1216%26h%3D912&w=3840&q=75)

Begin with precise character movements to amplify user engagement. Research shows that 78% of users report stronger emotional connections when interactions are fluid and natural. Utilize a blend of speed and anticipation to create tension and release, stimulating excitement or calmness as needed.

Incorporate sound intricately, as auditory cues can enhance sensory immersion. A study found that 67% of participants react more positively to experiences that combine visual and audio elements, particularly when the audio complements the actions on-screen. The synchronization of sound and visuals can trigger specific responses, guiding the user’s emotional state toward desired outcomes.

Utilize storytelling techniques to create a narrative arc that resonates. Data indicates that narratives can improve retention by up to 65%, making the interaction more memorable. Structure the plot with relatable characters and conflicts to capture users’ attention, facilitating a deeper bond with the virtual environment.

Experiment with color theory to evoke psychological reactions. For instance, blue hues typically induce tranquility, while reds can generate urgency. Research highlights that about 90% of snap judgments about a product or environment are influenced by color alone, making it a powerful tool in context-building.

Monitor user feedback meticulously to refine the experience continuously. Analysis shows that environments which adapt according to user responses have a 42% higher satisfaction rate. Implementing AI-driven feedback loops can provide insights into emotional responses, allowing for more tailored interactions.

## Understanding Emotional Responses in VR

![Understanding Emotional Responses in VR](/uploads/images/designing-emotion-through-animation-in-virtual-reality-creating-immers-tdwoa0za.webp)

![Understanding Emotional Responses in VR](/uploads/images/designing-emotion-through-animation-in-virtual-reality-creating-immers-tdwoa0za.webp)

Quantifiable data indicates that users in immersive environments report a 70% increase in emotional engagement compared to traditional media. This heightened engagement can be attributed to the sensation of presence, where users feel as though they are truly part of the experience. Techniques such as spatial audio, realistic visuals, and interactivity play significant roles in amplifying psychological responses.

Research reveals that scenarios designed to elicit sadness can reduce heart rate variability by up to 30%, illustrating a deep physiological effect aligned with emotional states. This response can be utilized intentionally to create impactful narratives. For developers looking to integrate such depth into projects, [hire breeze js developers](https://moldstud.com/articles/p-how-can-i-hire-a-pug-developer-for-my-project) who specialize in crafting compelling interactions that resonate emotionally.

Furthermore, studies show that positive emotional stimuli can enhance recall by 60%. Implementing feedback loops, where users' choices influence outcomes, reinforces emotional weight, creating memorable moments. For business leaders aiming to integrate analytics with user experience, seeking expertise can streamline this process. Consider partnering with experts and [hire business intelligence consultants](https://moldstud.com/articles/p-unveiling-the-diversity-of-business-intelligence-consulting-services-available-to-companies) to analyze user data and refine emotional engagement strategies effectively.

Incorporating these findings into design practices expands the capacity for connection and response in transient experiences, ultimately fostering an environment that captivates and resonates beyond the initial interaction.

### The Science of Emotion in Immersive Environments

Utilize data indicating that emotional responses can be manipulated within interactive settings, where users report up to 70% higher levels of engagement compared to non-interactive formats. Tailor sensory stimuli such as sound and visuals to resonate with user experiences, leading to heightened feelings of involvement.

Quantitative studies reveal that environments designed to evoke specific emotional states can influence physiological reactions. For example, a study conducted by the University of California found that immersive soundscapes can reduce anxiety levels by 43%, as measured by heart rate variability. Implement auditory cues designed to elicit nostalgia or joy, promoting deeper user connection.

Research indicates that spatial presence is a key factor in emotional response; 85% of participants in controlled trials reported feeling more connected to the experience when the environment simulated real-world physics. Create environments that mimic natural movements and responses to foster a sense of authenticity.

Leveraging user feedback loops enhances emotional resonance. Systems that adapt based on biometric data, such as facial recognition or heart rate monitors, achieve a breakthrough in personal relevance, increasing satisfaction and immersion rates by up to 50%. Integrate feedback mechanisms to adjust experiences in real-time based on emotional state.

Explore narrative structures that evoke empathy; studies show that storytelling within these contexts can enhance emotional impact. A report by the Nielsen Company demonstrated that stories lead to a 20% increase in memory retention, crucial for building user attachment to the virtual world.

Implementing activities that require choice significantly boosts emotional investment. Research indicates that when participants engage in decision-making processes, emotional responses can amplify by over 60%. Craft scenarios that allow users to influence outcomes, making them integral to the storyline.

Consider the role of social interactions; environments that support multi-user experiences can amplify sentiments of joy and belonging. Data suggests that group interactions within an immersive setup can enhance overall emotional satisfaction, with a 30% increase in reported positive emotions compared to solitary engagements.

### Case Studies: Successful Emotional VR Experiences

One exceptional case is "Beat Saber," a rhythm game that utilizes immersive environments to evoke excitement and joy. Statistics reveal that it has sold over 4 million copies across various platforms. Players experience a blend of music and movement, fueling endorphins and enhancing mood through gameplay.

Another notable example is "Half + Half," a social VR application that has proven to foster connection among users. With over 500,000 downloads, it enables virtual interactions, allowing individuals to experience feelings of companionship and belonging. In studies, players reported a 70% increase in feelings of social connectivity compared to traditional gaming experiences.

"Wander," a VR exploration app designed for travel enthusiasts, exemplifies the power of escapism. By allowing users to virtually visit real-world locations, it not only satisfies curiosity but also generates a strong sense of nostalgia. Research indicates that 68% of users felt an emotional uplift after using this platform, discussing the calming effects associated with virtual travel.

For developers interested in creating such compelling platforms, utilizing strategies from other industries can enhance effectiveness. Consider enhancing the narrative structure by integrating engaging backstories or character arcs, as seen in successful film and gaming sectors. Collaborative efforts might also yield fruitful results; for instance, teams focusing on backend development can significantly improve the overall user experience. If you are looking for specialized talent, you can [hire remote node js developer](https://moldstud.com/articles/p-where-can-i-find-top-remote-nodejs-developers) or [hire remote elixir developers](https://moldstud.com/articles/p-how-to-find-remote-elixir-developer-jobs) to bring innovative ideas to life.

Storytelling remains a powerful tool in these immersive environments. Studies have shown that narratives can elevate user engagement by up to 30%. Coupling this with rich sensory feedback is pivotal in crafting impactful virtual scenarios. Current data suggests that an emotionally charged narrative increases user retention and satisfaction significantly.

### Measuring Emotional Impact: Tools and Techniques

![Measuring Emotional Impact: Tools and Techniques](/uploads/images/designing-emotion-through-animation-in-virtual-reality-creating-immers-toa7plew.webp)

![Measuring Emotional Impact: Tools and Techniques](/uploads/images/designing-emotion-through-animation-in-virtual-reality-creating-immers-toa7plew.webp)

Implement biometric tools such as eye tracking and galvanic skin response (GSR) to gather physiological data. These technologies can provide actionable insights by monitoring user reactions in real time.

Incorporate qualitative methods such as surveys and interviews post-experience. Utilize standardized questionnaires like the Positive and Negative Affect Schedule (PANAS) or the Emotional Response Questionnaire (ERQ) for structured feedback.

Leverage machine learning algorithms for sentiment analysis on user-generated content. Analyzing comments and reviews can reveal trends in emotional responses.

Utilize physiological metrics to correlate emotional responses with engagement. For example, heart rate variability (HRV) is indicative of emotional regulation and can be tracked using wearables.

Benchmark findings against industry standards. According to a 2024 report by PwC, 73% of consumers indicate that emotional connection influences their decisions. Use this data to contextualize and assess your project’s impact.

Finally, collaborate with behavioral scientists to interpret findings. Combining expertise can enhance understanding of emotional impacts and inform design decisions moving forward.

## Techniques for Crafting Emotion through Animation

Utilize the principles of **character design** effectively. Research indicates that characters with exaggerated features, such as large eyes or expressive facial expressions, resonate more deeply with audiences. For instance, studies show that characters with high emotional expressiveness can increase engagement by up to 70%.

Incorporate **color psychology** strategically. Colors evoke specific feelings; for example, warm tones like red and orange can stimulate excitement, while cooler shades such as blue and green often evoke calmness. A survey found that color choice can influence viewer perception and emotional response, with 85% of respondents agreeing that color affects their mood while viewing animated content.

Implement **sound design** as a complementary aspect. The right audio cues can amplify emotional responses significantly. Research shows that music paired with visual stimuli can enhance empathy and connection, boosting emotional impact by up to 50% compared to visuals alone.

Leverage **timing and pacing**. Fast movements and quick cuts can create a sense of urgency or excitement, while slow transitions evoke contemplation and sadness. Experts suggest that varying pacing throughout a scene can lead to heightened emotional states, impacting viewer retention by 40%.

Experiment with **narrative structures**. Non-linear story arcs or unexpected plot twists can create suspense, thereby intensifying emotional engagement. Data reveals that stories with complex narrative structures result in a 30% increase in audience attachment to characters.

Finally, embrace **body language manipulation**. Subtle animations reflecting posture, gestures, and movements can convey rich emotional nuances. Studies indicate that audiences are more likely to empathize with characters exhibiting realistic body language, improving emotional connection by approximately 60%.

### Character Design and Animation Styles

Focus on distinctive features and meaningful backstories for characters to enhance relatability. A well-defined character can significantly boost user engagement; studies indicate that users retain 55% more information from experiences with relatable figures.

Integrate cultural elements into character design. Customizing traits based on user demographics can improve connection rates. For instance, characters reflecting specific cultural backgrounds have shown to increase relevancy and immersion by up to 38%.

### Animation Techniques

### Style Variations

Tailor character design and animation styles based on target audience research. Analytics reveal that 78% of high-engagement experiences are driven by suitable character appeal. Constantly iterate based on user feedback, an approach that has proven to enhance user satisfaction by 60% in recent studies.

### Utilizing Soundscapes to Enhance Emotional Depth

Integrating soundscapes can significantly amplify the emotional resonance of any immersive setting. A study by the University of California found that environments enriched with tailored audio elements increased user satisfaction by 70%. To effectively implement soundscapes, prioritize dynamic range and stereo placement, which allows sound to evoke spatial recognition and emotional response.

Use ambient sounds that reflect the thematic elements of your scene. For instance, nature sounds like rustling leaves or distant birds create tranquility, enhancing feelings of serenity. Conversely, sharp, discordant noises can evoke tension and anxiety, particularly in suspenseful situations. According to research conducted by the Journal of Experimental Psychology, sound frequencies can influence emotional arousal, so carefully select frequencies that resonate with your emotional objectives.

Consider implementing reactive sound design, where audio evolves based on user interactions. For example, in a virtual forest, louder footsteps or snapping branches can alert users to approaching dangers, heightening anxiety. Notably, a survey by the Interactive Audio Symposium indicated that 85% of respondents found interactive auditory cues increased their investment in the experience.

Furthermore, collaborate with sound designers to create original sound palettes tailored to your narrative. Incorporating unique sound elements can cultivate authenticity. Remember that repetitive sounds may decrease engagement; therefore, diversity in sound layering is crucial. Effective soundscapes can be a mix of diegetic sounds–those originating within the environment–and non-diegetic sounds, such as a musical score that underscores critical emotional moments.

Finally, test your soundscapes rigorously. A/B testing various audio configurations can reveal which combinations elicit stronger emotional reactions. By grounding your approach in research and user feedback, you can refine your audio landscape to effectively engage your audience on an emotional level.

### Spatial Storytelling: Creating a Sense of Place

Focus on the physicality of environments by using scale and perspective. An appropriate use of spatial dimensions helps the user to understand their place within the virtual setting. For example, a hallway that visually stretches or compresses changes audience perception significantly. Adjusting the height and width of objects can also influence the emotional response and spatial awareness.

Layer narrative elements within the environment. By strategically placing visual cues or audio whispers throughout a space, users can piece together a story as they explore. Research indicates that retaining attention within an interactive setting is enhanced by 30% when narrative threads are woven into the physical experience.

Utilize sound design to reinforce location impact. Spatial audio can provide depth, guiding attention and enhancing the sense of presence. For instance, directional sounds can prompt users towards specific objects or areas, encouraging them to engage with the environment more fully. Studies show that incorporating 3D audio elements increases immersion perception by nearly 40%.

Consider context with environmental storytelling. Every object or design choice should reflect the narrative's themes. Utilize textures and materials relevant to the story's context. Research from the Interpersonal Communication Journal highlights that users form emotional attachments to spaces which resonate with their past experiences by 25% more than generic designs.

| Design Element | Impact on User Engagement (%) |
| --- | --- |
| Spatial Dimensions | 30% |
| Narrative Integration | 30% |
| Spatial Audio | 40% |
| Contextual Storytelling | 25% |

Incorporate interaction elements that allow users to alter the environment. Interactive components can deepen engagement, providing a platform for personal connection. Research by the Interaction Design Foundation finds that environments responsive to user actions can boost engagement metrics by 50% or more. Encourage exploration with tasks that reward discovery, making users active participants in the spatial narrative.

Finally, ensure transitions between different spaces are fluid. Smooth movement increases the feeling of connectedness between locations. Avoid abrupt changes that can disrupt users’ immersion. The Journal of Virtual Environments emphasizes that seamless transitions are a significant factor in maintaining user immersion, influencing a sense of continuity by 35%.

### Feedback Loops: Adjusting Emotions in Real-Time

**Integrating real-time feedback mechanisms enhances engagement.** Utilize physiological responses such as heart rate or skin conductance to gauge participant emotions during sessions. Research from Stanford University indicates that real-time adjustments can elevate user satisfaction by up to 30%.

Implement adaptive soundscapes. When stress levels rise, shift ambient sounds toward calming frequencies, which has been shown to decrease anxiety by 25%, according to a study in the Journal of Sound and Vibration. This auditory manipulation not only alters sentiment but also maintains user focus.

**Utilize choice architecture** to offer users agency over their experiences. A randomized trial showed that when users selected from multiple narrative paths, emotional engagement rose by 40%. This empowerment helps them connect more deeply with the presented scenarios.

Facilitate immediate feedback dialogues through avatars. An analysis published in the International Journal of Human-Computer Studies found that avatars responding to user emotions increased perceived realism and satisfaction by 20%. Program these avatars to react not only to user input but also to surrounding virtual events, fostering a more believable interaction.

Analytics play a pivotal role in assessing emotional responses. Track engagement metrics and emotion-related data to ascertain patterns. Insights gleaned from user behavior can guide iterative improvements, solidifying experience offerings with a 15% increase in retention rates as reported by the User Experience Professionals Association.

Lastly, harness machine learning algorithms to predict user emotional trajectories based on interaction data. A predictive model can inform dynamic environmental changes, thereby enhancing user immersion by approximately 35%. This approach not only maximizes engagement but ensures that emotional arcs remain compelling throughout the interaction.

### Experimenting with Color and Visual Effects

Utilize a color palette that reflects the intended mood. Research indicates that 90% of snap judgments on products are based on color alone, highlighting its power. For instance, warm hues such as red and orange evoke energy, while cool tones like blue and green foster calmness.

Incorporate light effects to enhance realism. Soft shadows can create depth, whereas dynamic lighting changes can simulate emotional states. For instance, a gradual dimming can evoke tension, contrasting with sudden bright flashes that may surprise users.

Leverage color theory fundamentals. Complementary colors combined can create visual tension, fostering a feeling of excitement. Analyze case studies where successful projects employed contrasting palettes to maximize user engagement, observing a reported increase in time spent in the virtual space by up to 30%.

Experiment with saturation levels. Highly saturated colors are attention-grabbing, yet using desaturated tones can evoke nostalgia or tranquility. Balancing saturation can guide users' focus toward critical elements within the experience.

Incorporate slow transitions in color changes. Abrupt shifts may disorient users, while gradual blending enhances visual cohesion and can subtly influence emotional responses. Studies suggest users retain more information when color transitions last at least 250 milliseconds.

Utilize visual metaphors through color coding. Associating specific colors with particular actions or emotions improves recall and comprehension. Ensure consistency across interactions to reinforce user expectations and deepen understanding of the experience's narrative.

Consider using industry-standard color contrast checkers to ensure accessibility. Approximately 1 in 12 men and 1 in 200 women experience color vision deficiency. Ensuring a broad audience can engage meaningfully increases inclusivity in participation rates by nearly 20%.

Experiment with textures and patterns alongside colors. Visual complexity can create a sense of richness and immersion, with studies showing textured backgrounds can improve user satisfaction by 15%, increasing the likelihood of return visits.

Create a feedback loop by incorporating user responses to visual elements. A/B testing different color schemes and effects can yield insights into user preferences, guiding further iterations that optimize both engagement and emotional impact.

--- SOURCE 5: A Sense of Place: VR Journalism and Emotional Engagement ---
URL: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237

SUMMARY:
Drawing on the theory of spatial narrative, documentary and cinema literature and studies on media morality, this article examines the meaning of place in VR news stories and its ability to engage the user with the story. VR journalism alters the way we consider or create a sense of place in news stories (Sandvik Citation 2010, 139; Tricart Citation 2018). In VR journalism, place serves multiple functions, from being the principal focus of a story to embodying symbolic meaning and reinforcing emotions (Ryan, Foote, and Azaryahu Citation 2016; Tricart Citation 2018). Journalism scholars have become increasingly interested in place from the perspective of engaging experiences (e.g., Oppegaard and Rabby Citation 2016; Schmitz Weiss Citation 2015; Usher Citation 2019).

ABSTRACT
--------

Virtual reality (VR) and other immersive technologies introduce new opportunities for emotionally compelling narratives and user agency. Virtually mediated environments lie at the heart of immersive journalism (IJ) experiences, foregrounding a sense of presence and bridging the connection between the user and the character. Mediated environments in VR stories provide more than a setting since the user can interact with and respond to the surroundings. Drawing on the theory of spatial narrative, documentary and cinema literature and studies on media morality, this article examines the meaning of place in VR news stories and its ability to engage the user with the story. This study contributes to the discussion of creating and communicating places in journalism studies by examining spatial storytelling in immersive news stories, which are available in the NYT VR smartphone application. This paper argues that spatial storytelling eventually affects what is experienced and how it is experienced either by demonstrating the circumstances with aesthetical elements or via the selection of spaces.

KEYWORDS:

*   [Emotion](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/Emotion)
*   [engagement](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/engagement)
*   [immersive journalism](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/immersive+journalism)
*   [place](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/place)
*   [spatial narrative](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/spatial+narrative)
*   [virtual reality](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/virtual+reality)
*   [close reading](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/close+reading)
*   [news](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/news)

Introduction
------------

Virtual reality (VR) creates the illusion of an accessible place that provides a visceral experience and opportunity for exploration. In VR journalism, spatial narrative is achieved by combining elements from video games and cinema using a “linear description” of events as seen in documentaries and the “non-linear exploration” which is common to video games (Nitsche [Citation 2008](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#), 79). What makes VR journalism different is that, rather than being about “storytelling”, it is about “story-living”, as it allows the user to have an active role (Maschio [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). In this paper, the idea of “spatial storytelling” refers to the ways VR news is constructed and “read” as stories that allow spatial exploration (Jenkins [Citation 2004](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)).

Many definitions of place and space exist, which sometimes overlap and contradict each other. When we discuss “place”, we refer to “real” and built locations. Place has a history, a memory and a sense of community (Ryan [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#), 89). We understand place as being “an agentic player” (Gieryn [Citation 2000](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#), 466) in VR stories, rather than merely a backdrop to action and characters (see also Usher [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). VR journalism alters the way we consider or create a sense of place in news stories (Sandvik [Citation 2010](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#), 139; Tricart [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). In VR journalism, place serves multiple functions, from being the principal focus of a story to embodying symbolic meaning and reinforcing emotions (Ryan, Foote, and Azaryahu [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Tricart [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). Space refers to the digital environment and its ability to narrate the events and cater for different forms of agency (de la Peña et al. [Citation 2010](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Tuan [Citation 2011](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). VR simulates a sense of place by manifesting “real” places as digital spaces (Usher [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)).

The use of immersive and interactive storytelling is growing, and news sources (e.g., _USA Today_, CNN and _The Guardian_) are experimenting with VR journalism. The _New York Times_ (NYT) launched VR journalism in 2015 with the publication of the documentary _The Displaced_ (Hopkins 2017). However, the biggest challenges posed by VR are technical and include problems with producing and streaming large multimedia files. Yet, many news organisations are currently investing in 5G technology, which is expected to provide internet speed more than 20 times faster than 4G technology and will, therefore, offer the possibility to expand workflow, user experience and narrative possibilities (see Wasserman, Parr, and Kenol [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); WARC [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)).

Discussion of the potential of immersive media to create engagement with others has become more prevalent in recent years (e.g., de la Peña et al. [Citation 2010](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Jones [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Nash [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Sánchez Laws [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). Studies on VR journalism have highlighted IJ’s ability to offer strong emotional experiences by allowing users to experience stories from a first-person perspective by inhabiting the story’s setting (de la Peña et al. [Citation 2010](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Kool [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Maschio [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Sánchez Laws [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Sundar, Kang, and Oprean [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). This paper contributes to the ongoing discussion of the connection between simulated presence and emotional engagement by examining how the user–space interaction allowed by VR news supports emotional responses. Here, we examine eight VR stories produced and/or distributed by the NYT and offer a typology of different forms of user–space interaction that support emotional engagement.

This paper focuses on the aesthetic and symbolic qualities of spatial storytelling in VR journalism, focusing on its potential to evoke emotional responses toward the characters’ suffering. In this context, by aesthetic, we mean stylistic choices in designing the space of the VR story, including lighting, mise en scène, framing and editing. David Fenner has stated that aesthetic is “a modifier of _attitude_” ([Citation 2008](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#), 18), referring to aesthetics’ capacity to influence the process of narrative understanding and atmosphere. The symbolic qualities of narrating place constitute a deeper social, political or cultural meaning, and these symbols often require activity from the user to realise and interpret them (Whitehead [Citation 1985](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#), 9).

Proximity to Place
------------------

Place is a significant component of journalism practices because it defines journalism’s cultural authority and the value of specific news events. How geographically or culturally proximate or distant the events are perceived shapes “what is included, what is excluded and why” (O’Neill and Harcup [Citation 2008](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#), 162). Journalism scholars have become increasingly interested in place from the perspective of engaging experiences (e.g., Oppegaard and Rabby [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Schmitz Weiss [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Usher [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). Although there is confusion about the meaning of audience engagement (it can be conceptualised from an audience-oriented perspective as attentiveness and participation or from a production-oriented perspective as journalistic techniques), engagement is often connected to close proximity to or an affective relationship with a place.

Proximity has been understood as a journalistic strategy to signal authority (Allan [Citation 2013](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Usher [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Zelizer [Citation 1990](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). Hence, news organisations aim to amplify audiences’ experiences of space–time proximity through reporting techniques such as constructed “on the spot” reporting (Huxford [Citation 2007](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)) or the use of citizens’ eyewitness imagery that accentuates an embodied sense of being at the scene of events as they happen (Ahva and Hellman [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Ahva and Pantti [Citation 2014](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Usher [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Zelizer [Citation 2007](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). Distance, accordingly, is associated with distrust and a lack of an emotional bond (Peters [Citation 2011](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#), 34). Distance not only refers to geographical or social distance but also to moral distance from characters and their situations. What Silverstone ([Citation 2007](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)) defined as a “proper distance” refers to the level of proximity, which requires the user to emotionally and morally engage with the issue and the characters involved (Silverstone [Citation 2007](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)).

Immersing in Narrative Spaces
-----------------------------

As discussed previously, place refers to factual and inter-textual qualities, whereas space alludes to the digital environment in which the user is immersed. Immersion has been understood as fostering “a deep connection” between the user and the virtual environment (Calleja [Citation 2014](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#), 25; Jones [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); McRoberts [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). This connection is enabled by VR technology as well as narrative and interactive qualities in the story-world (Cummings and Bailenson [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Slater and Wilbur [Citation 1997](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). It is this opportunity for immersion and exploring the environment that allegedly engages the user with the story and can create a sense of being there in the virtual environment (Jenkins [Citation 2004](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). We will briefly discuss presence and narrative qualities of space in immersing users in VR.

Presence refers to a sense of “being there” in the virtual environment as well as being responsive to the environment (IJsselsteijn and Giuseppe [Citation 2003](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#), 5). During presence, the users become less aware of the technology, which enables the perception that they are in the space and are experiencing the events directly. The fundamental purpose of IJ is to experience space as real-life characters would, creating an illusion that the events are more unfiltered than the broadcast news (Bolter and Grusin [Citation 2001](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#); Maschio [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). This sense of presence is both subjective and objective: subjectivity refers to the way users feel about being in the virtual environment, and objectivity involves the way they behave similarly to real-life situations (Sirkkunen et al. [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)).

Nonetheless, the notion of being there is only one presence-related cognitive factor. The level of interaction and realism applied in the VR story affects the user’s perceptions and emotional engagement in IJ (Sundar, Kang, and Oprean [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). Both the VR environment’s responsiveness to the user’s movements and social interaction occurring during the experience can intensify the user presence (Heeter [Citation 1992](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)).

In addition to contributing to a sense of presence, space has a key role in framing an understanding of narrative meanings. In VR, space is addressed before the overall understanding of the scene is established. From a narrative perspective, orientation into a new scene follows a clear pattern: when a scene changes, the users are required to reinvent themselves in the new space and gain an understanding of where they are located (Tricart [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#), 94). This draws the user’s attention to spatial cues in order to make sense of the surroundings. In film studies, space is also understood to reflect a character’s psychological attributes, adjust the mood and contribute to the overall narrative by providing interpretations of the story (Bordwell, Staiger, and Thompson [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)).

Emotional Engagement in VR
--------------------------

In this section, we will review the research on emotional engagement in IJ. The purpose of storytelling is to engage the audience in the narrative. Aesthetic elements create an atmosphere and convey feelings, which guide how the story is experienced (Bordwell and Thompson [Citation 2010](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)). McRoberts ([Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17512786.2020.1799237#)

enriched_points.expanded_content: VR storytelling immerses users in interactive narratives, fostering active participation and deeper focus, leading to heightened engagement compared to traditional media.
By facilitating personal experiences, VR enables users to forge strong emotional bonds with characters and narratives, resulting in lasting impressions and empathy.
VR allows for non-linear narratives and spatial storytelling, giving creators the freedom to experiment with immersive formats that enhance user experience.
The accessibility of VR experiences attracts diverse audiences, making storytelling more inclusive and promoting engagement across various demographics and cultural backgrounds.
VR storytelling transcends traditional boundaries, offering unique perspectives on cultural narratives and fostering understanding through shared immersive experiences.
